Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

Now I see the structure more clearly. There are vertical connections between the rings! Let me map out the full structure:

- Row 29: 2 blocks (ring 2 bottom)

These rings are connected vertically at cols 23 and 35 by blocks at rows 20, 23, 32, 41.
When I click a red block, it rotates the horizontal ring that the red block is adjacent to. But the rotation might also affect connected rings through the vertical connections.
Wait, but I clicked ring 1's red block and ring 1 rotated. Let me check if ring 4 also rotated.
Actually, let me reconsider. The red blocks are at:

- Ring 3: (34, 13) - left of ring 3
Each red block is associated with a specific ring. When I click a red block, it rotates that ring CW.
But when I clicked ring 1's red block, ring 2 and ring 3 also changed. This suggests that the rings are interconnected.
Let me look at the vertical connections more carefully. The blocks at cols 23 and 35 form vertical paths:
- Col 23: row 17 (ring 1) -> row 20 -> row 23 -> row 26 (ring 2) -> row 29 -> row 32 -> row 35 (ring 3) -> row 38 -> row 41 -> row 44 (ring 4)
- Col 35: row 17 (ring 1) -> row 20 -> row 23 -> row 26 (ring 2) -> row 29 -> row 32 -> row 35 (ring 3) -> row 38 -> row 41 -> row 44 (ring 4)
So the structure is like a ladder with two vertical rails at cols 23 and 35, and horizontal rungs at rows 17, 26, 35, and 44.
When I click a red block, it might rotate the entire ladder structure, not just one ring. But that doesn't explain the changes I saw.
Let me re-examine the changes more carefully. After clicking ring 2's red block:
- Ring 2 top row: [w, b, b, w, S, p, g, S, g, g] -> [b, b, w, S, p, g, S, g, g, w] (CW rotation)
- Ring 2 bottom: (29,23)=w, (29,35)=w (no change)
- Ring 1: no change
- Ring 3: no change
After clicking ring 1's red block:

- Ring 2 top row: [b, b, w, S, p, g, S, g, g, w] -> [b, b, g, S, p, g, w, g, g, w]
  This is NOT a simple CW rotation.
- Ring 2 bottom: (29,35)=w -> (29,35)=g (changed!)
- Ring 3 top row: [w, S, b, w, b, S, p, b, g, S] -> [w, S, w, w, b, S, w, b, g, S]

Maybe the goal is to rotate the rings so that the yellow block in ring 1 aligns with the yellow markers in rings 2 and 3? But the yellow block in ring 1 is at col 32, and the markers in rings 2 and 3 are at col 35. These don't align.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me check ring 4 (row 44) and the vertical connectors
seg = current_frame.segmentation

# Get all blocks in the vertical columns (cols 23 and 35)
print("--- Col 23 blocks ---")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    c1 = min(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])
    r1 = min(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])
    if c1 == 23 and 15 <= r1 <= 45:
        print(f"  ({r1}, {c1}): {n['color']}, px={n['pixels']}")

print("\n--- Col 35 blocks ---")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    c1 = min(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])
    r1 = min(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])
    if c1 == 35 and 15 <= r1 <= 45:
        print(f"  ({r1}, {c1}): {n['color']}, px={n['pixels']}")

print("\n--- Ring 4 (row 44) ---")
blocks_44 = []
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] == 4:
        r1 = min(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])
        c1 = min(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])
        if r1 == 44:
            blocks_44.append((c1, n['color']))
blocks_44.sort()
for c, col in blocks_44:
    print(f"  col {c}: {col}")

# Click ring 3 red block to see what happens
print("\n--- Clicking ring 3 red block ---")
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 34, 'col': 15}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me check ring 4 (row 44) and the vertical connectors
seg = current_frame.segmentation

# Get all blocks in the vertical columns (cols 23 and 35)
print("--- Col 23 blocks ---")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    c1 = min(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])
    r1 = min(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])
    if c1 == 23 and 15 <= r1 <= 45:
        print(f"  ({r1}, {c1}): {n['color']}, px={n['pixels']}")

print("\n--- Col 35 blocks ---")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    c1 = min(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])
    r1 = min(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])
    if c1 == 35 and 15 <= r1 <= 45:
        print(f"  ({r1}, {c1}): {n['color']}, px={n['pixels']}")

print("\n--- Ring 4 (row 44) ---")
blocks_44 = []
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] == 4:
        r1 = min(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])
        c1 = min(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])
        if r1 == 44:
            blocks_44.append((c1, n['color']))
blocks_44.sort()
for c, col in blocks_44:
    print(f"  col {c}: {col}")

# Click ring 3 red block to see what happens
print("\n--- Clicking ring 3 red block ---")
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 34, 'col': 15}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
